feed.title: Combined summary - What if we let Quantum Hunters get Bitcoin rewards ?

entry.title: Combined summary - What if we let Quantum Hunters get Bitcoin rewards ?
entry.summary: In a recent exchange on the Bitcoin Development Mailing List, Amon introduced an alternative to BIP-361's coin freeze proposal, aimed at addressing the potential threat of quantum computing to Bitcoin. His draft Informational BIP suggests setting up a system where the quantum capture of exposed coins is transformed into a self-financing prize mechanism, which he believes would be the largest open technological prize in history. This new framework consists of three main components: the Genesis Quantum Transaction (GQT), which acknowledges the first successful spend from a quantum-vulnerable address; the Quantum Vault (QV), which automatically collects 90% of the captured funds and distributes them periodically to proven quantum actors in sync with the Bitcoin halving cycle; and the Quantum Proof Address (QPA), an on-chain identity for any actor demonstrating quantum capture capability. This setup aims to align long-term ecosystem stability by offering immediate rewards and ongoing distributions from the Vault, rather than incentivizing quick liquidation of stolen funds. The full draft of this proposal can be reviewed [here](https://github.com/amonmoce/Hunting-The-Bitcoin-One-Piece/blob/master/bip-hunting-the-bitcoin-one-piece.md). The dialogue around this proposal has sparked varied feedback. Nikita expressed skepticism regarding the motivation for participation under this framework, questioning why individuals would opt for rewards over outright theft of funds. In response, Amon clarified that the proposed mechanism does not allow for simple theft; instead, it ensures that anyone with quantum capabilities who engages will receive a structured reward, with the major portion of funds sequestered for future distribution, thus promoting responsible quantum capability demonstration. Another contributor, Ronak, while appreciative of the creativity of the "One Piece" concept and its theoretical merits, voiced concerns about the practical implications, especially concerning the roughly 6.7 million BTC that could become vulnerable to quantum attacks. He highlighted the unpredictability of quantum computing's advancement and suggested that the incentive structure might overly entice attackers, risking Bitcoin's long-term stability and value. In subsequent communications, Amon further explained that his proposal assumes the implementation of BIP-360, making all new addresses quantum-resistant and putting the onus on holders of old addresses to transition to these new, secure addresses. He described a scenario where each vulnerable unspent transaction output (UTXO) represents a "bitcoin one piece" opportunity, potentially diffusing risks by not having the same individual capture all vulnerable BTC, thereby preserving Bitcoin’s core principles and allowing hunters to earn their rewards similarly to how miners are compensated.
